title: Maximum Sum of Subsequence With Non-adjacent Elements
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: Pareto-optimal challenge: the single C++ solution is also the speed extreme and the memory extreme. Per the repo standard (no coincident files), only solution.cpp is shipped; there is no separate solution-runtime or solution-memory file.

variants.languageReason: The workload is up to 5e4 online point updates over a fixed-state DP. C++ keeps the segment-tree merge as inlined arithmetic over contiguous memory, avoids VM/interpreter/GC overhead, and uses 64-bit integers for exact intermediate sums up to about 5e9.
variants.approachJustification: Represent each value as a max-plus 2x2 matrix for the house-robber transition. Segment concatenation is associative matrix composition, so an iterative segment tree updates one leaf and recomposes O(log n) ancestors. This same implementation is the fastest practical and memory-conscious accepted approach.

reasoningSummary: The static no-adjacent-subsequence maximum is the house-robber DP. Each array element is a max-plus transition from previous-selected/not-selected states, and adjacent intervals compose associatively. A segment tree over those four values supports arbitrary point updates and reads the whole-array answer from the root after each query.
